functions.method_signature: def __init__(self, flow_name: str, run_id: str, output_dir: Optional[str]):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow being executed

parameters.name: run_id
parameters.description: Unique identifier for this test run

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory where reports will be saved. Must not be None.

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the ReportsManager with flow name, run ID, and output directory. Raises ValueError if output_dir is None since reports require an explicit output directory. Creates the output directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs with exist_ok=True. Initializes self.steps as an empty list to hold ReportStep instances. Initializes self._executed_step_ids as an empty list to track which steps have been executed. Sets self._handler to None (will be populated in start()). Initializes self._built_index as an empty dictionary for fast step lookup by pattern prefix. Records self._suite_start as the current UTC datetime. Sets self._last_step_end_monotonic to None for duration tracking. Initializes self._exact_pattern_index as an empty dictionary for exact pattern matching. Creates self.console_buffer as a StringIO instance to capture console output. Sets stdout/stderr tee attributes to None. Sets self._log_capture_handler to None. Constructs self._flow_dir as the path to the test flow directory. Initializes self._dynamic_added as an empty set. Initializes self._location_index as an empty dictionary for file/line lookup. Sets self._next_exec_order to 1 for tracking execution sequence.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Starts the reporting system by scanning source code for expected steps and setting up log capture. First calls self.scan_sources() to parse test flow Python files and extract all framework_logger.info() calls as expected steps. Creates a _LogHandler instance passing self and adds it to framework_logger to intercept INFO messages. Creates a logging.Handler instance for capturing all log levels, sets its level to DEBUG, creates a formatter with timestamp and level, and defines a custom emit function that writes formatted log records to self.console_buffer. Adds this handler to framework_logger. Attempts to tee stdout and stderr by saving originals to self._orig_stdout and self._orig_stderr, creating _Tee instances that write to both original streams and console_buffer, and replacing sys.stdout and sys.stderr with the tee instances. Wraps stdout/stderr tee setup in exception handling and logs debug message if it fails. Logs a debug message indicating initialization is complete with the number of potential steps found and the report path.

functions.method_signature: def finalize(self, success: bool, exception: Optional[BaseException] = None):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: success
parameters.description: Whether the test flow completed successfully

parameters.name: exception
parameters.description: Exception that caused test failure, if any

functions.what_it_does: Finalizes the reporting system by marking incomplete steps, cleaning up handlers, restoring stdout/stderr, and generating reports. If success is False and there are executed steps, gets the last executed step and if its status is PASS or PENDING, changes it to FAIL. If an exception is provided, appends exception information to the step's logged_message (truncated to 500 characters). Iterates through all steps and changes any with status PENDING to NOT_EXECUTED. Removes the _LogHandler from framework_logger with exception handling. Removes the log capture handler from framework_logger with exception handling. Restores sys.stdout and sys.stderr to their original values with exception handling. Checks the ENABLE_HTML toggle and if True, calls self._generate_html() to create the HTML report. Checks the ENABLE_JSON toggle and if True, calls self._write_json() to create the JSON report. Logs an info message with the report path (or indicates HTML is disabled if ENABLE_HTML is False).

functions.method_signature: def record_step_execution(self, log_message: str, record: logging.LogRecord):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: log_message
parameters.description: The log message text to match against expected steps

parameters.name: record
parameters.description: The logging record containing pathname and lineno metadata

functions.what_it_does: Core method that matches logged messages to expected steps and tracks execution. Normalizes the log message by stripping whitespace. Extracts absolute file path and line number from the logging record. Imports time module for monotonic timing. Checks if the message looks like a retry attempt (contains ' - attempt ' or ends with ' successful'). If it's an attempt-like message and exists in exact_pattern_index, increments the occurrence count and updates timestamp, then returns. If pathname and lineno are available and exist in location_index, iterates through step IDs at that location and checks if the pattern matches - if found, calls _mark_step_execution and returns. Iterates through all steps with PENDING status and checks if the pattern matches using _match() - for patterns starting with '{…}', verifies the prefix matches before the ' - attempt ' part. If a match is found, adds the location to location_index and calls _mark_step_execution, then returns. Iterates through all steps (any status) and checks for pattern match with prefix verification for retry patterns - if found, increments occurrence count and updates timestamp, then returns. If no match found and the log came from a file, attempts to extract the logger call pattern from the source file at that line using _extract_logger_call_pattern(). If a pattern is extracted, creates a new ReportStep with status PASS, adds it to self.steps, updates indexes, and returns. If still no match and it's an attempt-like message, creates a new dynamic ReportStep with the message as the pattern, adds it to steps and indexes, and returns. This method handles both expected steps (from source scanning) and dynamic steps (discovered during execution).
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by _LogHandler.emit() when framework_logger.info() is invoked

functions.method_signature: def _mark_step_execution(self, step: ReportStep, normalized_msg: str, time_module):

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The ReportStep instance to mark as executed

parameters.name: normalized_msg
parameters.description: The normalized log message text

parameters.name: time_module
parameters.description: The time module for monotonic timing

functions.what_it_does: Marks a step as executed by updating its status, timestamp, and execution metadata. Gets the current UTC datetime and formats it as ISO string with microseconds and Z suffix. Gets the current monotonic time for duration calculation. If there are previously executed steps, gets the last executed step and if it has a start monotonic time but no duration, calculates the duration as the difference between current monotonic time and the previous step's start time in milliseconds. If the step's execution_order is None (first time being executed), assigns it the next execution order number, increments _next_exec_order, adds the step ID to _executed_step_ids, records the current monotonic time as _start_monotonic, and sets first_timestamp_dt to current datetime if not already set. Sets the step status to PASS. If the step does not have a logged_message yet, sets it to the normalized_msg. Updates the step timestamp to the current ISO timestamp. Increments the step occurrences count (or sets to 1 if it was 0 or None). This method is called whenever a log message matches an expected step pattern.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Scans test flow source code files to discover expected test steps by parsing framework_logger.info() calls. Creates a search_paths list containing self._flow_dir (the test flow directory). Initializes step_id to 1. Iterates through each path in search_paths, skipping if the path is not a directory. Walks the directory tree using os.walk() to find all files. For each file, skips if it does not end with .py. For Python files, reads the file content and parses it using ast.parse() to get the abstract syntax tree. Wraps parsing in exception handling to skip files that cannot be parsed. Walks the AST using ast.walk() to find all nodes. For each node, checks if it is an ast.Call node. Checks if the function being called is an attribute access where the object is 'framework_logger' and the attribute is 'info'. If so, checks if there are arguments to the call. Extracts the pattern from the first argument using self._extract_pattern(). If a pattern is extracted, computes the relative file path from Constants.ROOT_DIR, creates a new ReportStep with the step_id, file path, line number, and pattern, appends it to self.steps, adds the step_id to _built_index using the first 10 characters of the pattern as the key, and increments step_id. This method populates self.steps with all expected steps before test execution begins.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: step_id
parameters.description: The 1-based step ID

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves a ReportStep instance by its 1-based step ID. Since step IDs are 1-based but the list is 0-indexed, subtracts 1 from step_id to get the correct index. Returns self.steps[step_id - 1]. This is a simple accessor method used throughout the class to get step instances by ID.

parameters.name: node
parameters.description: The AST node representing the first argument to framework_logger.info()

functions.what_it_does: Extracts the log message pattern from an AST node representing the argument to framework_logger.info(). If the node is an ast.Constant with a string value, returns the string value directly (this handles simple string literals like 'Login successful'). If the node is an ast.JoinedStr (f-string), iterates through the values in the f-string. For each value, if it is a Constant string, appends the string to parts. Otherwise (for interpolated expressions), appends the placeholder '{…}' to parts. Joins all parts and returns the resulting pattern string. Returns None if the node is neither a Constant nor a JoinedStr. This method converts f-strings like f'Clicked {button_name} button' into patterns like 'Clicked {…} button' that can be matched against actual log messages.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The expected pattern extracted from source code, may contain {…} placeholders

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The actual log message to match against the pattern

functions.what_it_does: Determines if a log message matches an expected pattern. First checks for exact equality - if pattern equals message, returns True. If both pattern and message contain ' - attempt ' (retry pattern), extracts the action part before ' - attempt ' from both, removes '{…}' placeholders from the pattern action, and checks if the pattern action (after stripping) is contained in the message action - returns True if so. Creates a simplified version of the pattern by removing all '{…}' placeholders and stripping whitespace. If the simplified pattern is non-empty and is contained in the message, returns True. Otherwise returns False. This method handles exact matches, f-string patterns with placeholders, and retry attempt patterns.
functions.usage_example:
if ReportsManager._match('Clicked {…} button', 'Clicked login button'):
    print('Match found')

functions.method_signature: def _extract_logger_call_pattern(self, file_path: str, lineno: int) -> Optional[str]:

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: Absolute path to the Python source file

parameters.name: lineno
parameters.description: Line number where the logger call occurred

functions.what_it_does: Attempts to extract the logger call pattern from a specific line in a source file. Wraps the entire operation in a try-except block that returns None on any exception. Opens the file and reads its content. Parses the content using ast.parse() to get the AST. Walks the AST to find all nodes. For each node, checks if it is an ast.Call and if its lineno matches the provided lineno. If so, checks if the function is an attribute access with attr 'info'. Checks if the value is a Name node with id 'framework_logger'. If all conditions match, checks if there are arguments and calls self._extract_pattern() on the first argument, returning the result. Returns None if no matching logger call is found at that line or if any exception occurs. This method is used to dynamically discover step patterns for log messages that were not found during the initial source scan.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Property that returns the full path to the HTML report file. Constructs the path by joining self.output_dir with 'report.html' using os.path.join(). This property provides a consistent way to access the report file path throughout the class.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Generates the comprehensive HTML test report with step table, statistics, and console output. Calculates summary statistics: total steps, passed steps, failed steps, and not executed steps. Gets the current UTC timestamp. Retrieves console output and metadata using _get_console_output(). Defines an esc() helper function that calls html.escape() with quote=True for HTML escaping. Initializes an empty rows_html list. Defines a status_color dictionary mapping status values to background colors (green for PASS, red for FAIL, gray for NOT_EXECUTED, white for PENDING). Separates steps into executed_steps (those with execution_order not None) and pending_steps (those with execution_order None). For executed steps, if first_timestamp_dt is None but timestamp exists, attempts to parse the timestamp string and set first_timestamp_dt. Sorts executed_steps by first_timestamp_dt and execution_order to get chronological order. Iterates through executed_steps and calculates duration for each step based on the time difference to the next step's first_timestamp_dt if the current step's duration_ms is None. Concatenates executed_steps and pending_steps to get ordered_steps. Creates exec_display_index mapping step IDs to their execution sequence numbers (1-based). Iterates through ordered_steps and for each step, constructs an HTML table row with background color based on status, displaying execution number, status, message (logged_message or pattern), file location, timestamp, duration, and occurrences. Constructs the complete HTML document string with DOCTYPE, head section containing title and embedded CSS styles for table formatting and filtering, body section with heading, summary statistics with color-coded badges, filter input box with JavaScript filterTable() function, step table with sticky header, console output section in a collapsible details element, and JavaScript for client-side table filtering. Writes the HTML document to self.report_path with UTF-8 encoding. Wraps file writing in exception handling that logs errors.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by finalize() if ENABLE_HTML is True

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Generates the companion JSON report with structured test execution data. Wraps the entire operation in a try-except block that logs warnings on failure. Constructs the JSON file path by joining self.output_dir with 'report.json'. Retrieves console output and metadata using _get_console_output(). Opens the JSON file for writing with UTF-8 encoding. Constructs a dictionary with keys: flow (flow name), run_id, generated_utc (current UTC timestamp in ISO format with Z suffix), steps (list of all steps converted to dictionaries using step.to_row()), and console (dictionary with truncated flag, byte count, line count, and content). Writes the dictionary to the JSON file using json.dump() with indent=2 for pretty formatting. Logs a warning if any exception occurs during JSON generation. This JSON report can be consumed by other tools for programmatic analysis of test results.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by finalize() if ENABLE_JSON is True

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves and optionally truncates the captured console output. Gets the raw console output by calling self.console_buffer.getvalue(). Initializes truncated flag to False. Encodes the raw string to UTF-8 bytes. Checks if the byte length exceeds CONSOLE_MAX_BYTES (2MB). If so, calculates head as 70% of max bytes and tail as 25% of max bytes. Extracts the head bytes and tail bytes from the raw bytes. Decodes head and tail to strings with errors='ignore' to handle incomplete UTF-8 sequences. Constructs the truncated output by concatenating head, a truncation marker line, and tail. Sets truncated flag to True. Counts the number of lines in the output by counting newline characters and adding 1 if the output does not end with a newline. Returns a tuple of (output_string, metadata_dict) where metadata contains truncated flag, byte count, and line count. This method ensures console output does not exceed size limits in the report.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: original
parameters.description: The original stream (sys.stdout or sys.stderr)

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: The StringIO buffer to capture output

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the _Tee instance by storing references to the original stream and the buffer. Sets self.original to the original stream and self.buf to the StringIO buffer. This allows the write() method to duplicate output to both destinations.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: The data to write to both streams

functions.what_it_does: Writes data to both the original stream and the buffer. First attempts to write data to self.original.write() wrapped in exception handling that silently passes on errors. Then attempts to write data to self.buf.write() wrapped in exception handling that silently passes on errors. This ensures output is duplicated to both destinations even if one write fails.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically when print() or sys.stdout.write() is invoked

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

functions.what_it_does: Flushes the original stream to ensure buffered output is written. Attempts to call self.original.flush() wrapped in exception handling that silently passes on errors. This method is required to fully implement the stream interface.
functions.usage_example: # Called automatically by the stream system

parameters.name: tag
parameters.description: HTML tag name of the element

parameters.name: href
parameters.description: href attribute value of the element

parameters.name: onclick
parameters.description: onclick attribute value of the element

functions.what_it_does: Determines the semantic type of an HTML element based on its tag name and attributes. Converts tag to uppercase. Checks if tag starts with 'H' and is 2 characters long with the second character being a digit (H1-H9) - returns 'Heading' if so. Checks if tag is 'A' and href is truthy - returns 'Link' if so. Checks if tag is 'BUTTON' or onclick is truthy - returns 'Button' if so. Checks if tag is 'INPUT', 'SELECT', or 'TEXTAREA' - returns 'FormControl' if so. Checks if tag is 'LABEL' - returns 'Label' if so. Checks if tag is 'IMG' - returns 'Image' if so. Otherwise returns the tag name in title case. This classification helps organize scraped elements by their functional role rather than just their HTML tag.

functions.method_signature:
def scrape_context(
    flow_name,
    context_name,
    page,
    excel_path,
    output_dir,
    screenshot_only=False,
    sub_context=None,
    lang_code=None,
    animations=False,
    target_dir=None
):

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow being scraped

parameters.name: context_name
parameters.description: Name of the page context being scraped (e.g., 'login_page', 'dashboard')

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page instance of the page to scrape

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the Excel workbook where scraped data will be written

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory where screenshots will be saved

parameters.name: screenshot_only
parameters.description: If True, only captures full-page screenshot without element scraping

parameters.name: sub_context
parameters.description: Optional sub-context name for organizing scraped data

parameters.name: lang_code
parameters.description: Language code for naming screenshot files

parameters.name: animations
parameters.description: If True, extracts and saves animated SVGs, GIFs, and visual animations

parameters.name: target_dir
parameters.description: Optional target directory (currently unused in implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Main scraping function that extracts UI elements and visual content from a web page and writes to Excel. Gets current timestamp in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format. Creates screenshots directory inside output_dir using ensure_dirs(). Constructs subctx_suffix by appending underscore and sub_context if provided. Calls save_full_page_screenshot() to capture full-page screenshot with naming pattern '{context_name}{subctx_suffix}_full_page'. Initializes empty elements list and seen set for deduplication. Adds a FullPage row to elements with context name, sub-context, page URL, timestamp, type 'FullPage', and screenshot filename. If screenshot_only is True, loads or creates the Excel workbook, writes the single FullPage row, logs completion message, and returns early. Otherwise, queries all elements in the page body using page.query_selector_all('body *'). For each element handle, evaluates the tag name and skips if tag is None or in Constants.IGNORE_TAGS. Checks if element is visible using is_visible() and skips if not. Gets inner text and strips whitespace, skipping if empty. Gets bounding box and skips if None or has zero width/height. Gets href and onclick attributes. Calls get_semantic_type() to classify the element. Creates dedup_key tuple of (sub_context, text, tag, href) and skips if already in seen set, otherwise adds to seen. Constructs screenshot filename with pattern '{context_name}{subctx_suffix}_{lang_code}_{index:04d}_{semantic_type}_{hash}'. Calls take_element_handle_screenshot() to capture element screenshot. Appends element data dictionary to elements list with all metadata fields. Wraps element processing in exception handling that logs debug messages for failures (except for 'Node is not an HTMLElement' errors which are silently skipped). If animations is True, creates animations directory, calls extract_animated_svgs() to get SVG animations, and for each SVG saves the GIF bytes if available and optionally saves the SVG XML (controlled by SAVE_ONLY_GIF_FROM_SVG toggle), adding rows to elements for each saved file. Calls extract_animated_gifs() to get GIF animations and saves each GIF file, adding rows to elements. Calls extract_animated_visuals() with selector '.stepsImage' to capture visual animations and adds rows to elements. Finally, loads or creates the Excel workbook and calls write_excel_context() to write all elements to the workbook. Logs completion message with element count and Excel path.
functions.usage_example:
scrape_context(
    flow_name='login_flow',
    context_name='login_page',
    page=page,
    excel_path='/path/to/baseline.xlsx',
    output_dir='/path/to/output',
    screenshot_only=False,
    lang_code='en-US',
    animations=True
)

parameters.name: cls
parameters.description: Class reference

functions.what_it_does: Resets all GlobalState class attributes to None or their default values. Sets stack, locale, language, printer_profile, easy_enroll, biz_model, region, country, country_code, language_code, address_payload, selected_printer_sku, tenant_email, flow_name, timestamp, mode, run_dir, target, payment_method, and simulator_platform to None. Sets headless to False. Sets collect_logs to False. Sets requirements to an empty dictionary. This method is called at the beginning of Config.init() to ensure a clean state before loading new configuration.

parameters.name: level
parameters.description: Logging level constant (e.g., logging.DEBUG, logging.INFO)

functions.what_it_does: Sets the logging level for the framework logger and all its handlers. Updates the global _LOG_LEVEL variable to the provided level. Calls framework_logger.setLevel(level) to set the logger's level. Iterates through all handlers attached to framework_logger and calls handler.setLevel(level) on each. This function allows dynamic adjustment of logging verbosity, typically called from the main entry point when --debug flag is provided.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Name of the logger

parameters.name: log_file
parameters.description: Filename for the log file

parameters.name: max_bytes
parameters.description: Maximum size of log file before rotation (5MB default)

parameters.name: backup_count
parameters.description: Number of backup log files to keep

functions.what_it_does: Creates and configures the framework logger with file and console handlers. Gets or creates a logger with the specified name using logging.getLogger(). Sets the logger level to _LOG_LEVEL (defaults to INFO). Sets propagate to False to prevent duplicate logging. Checks if the logger already has handlers and only adds handlers if none exist. Creates a formatter with pattern '[%(asctime)s] [%(levelname)s] %(message)s'. Constructs the log file path by joining Constants.LOG_DIR with log_file. Creates a RotatingFileHandler with the log file path, max_bytes size limit, backup_count for rotation, and UTF-8 encoding. Sets the file handler level to _LOG_LEVEL and applies the formatter. Adds the file handler to the logger. Creates a StreamHandler for console output, sets its level to _LOG_LEVEL, applies the formatter, and adds it to the logger. Returns the configured logger instance. This function is called once at module load time to create the framework_logger.
Lambda function that returns the current value of GlobalState.headless. Provides a functional interface to check if headless mode is enabled. Returns True if headless mode is on, False otherwise.
functions.usage_example:
if headless_on():
    print('Running in headless mode')

parameters.name: cls
parameters.description: Class reference

parameters.name: kwargs
parameters.description: Keyword arguments containing configuration values from command-line

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the framework configuration by populating GlobalState from provided keyword arguments and loading supporting data files. First calls GlobalState.reset() to clear any existing state. Extracts configuration values from kwargs and sets them on GlobalState: stack, locale, language, printer_profile (from 'profile' key), easy_enroll, biz_model (defaults to 'Flex'), headless (defaults to False), mode, flow_name, timestamp, run_dir, target, payment_method (defaults to 'credit_card_master'), simulator_platform, and requirements (defaults to empty dict). Calls cls._load_locale_info() to load locale data from locale_map.json and populate country, country_code, region, language, and language_code on GlobalState. Calls cls._load_address_data() to load address data from address_data.json and populate address_payload on GlobalState. Calls cls._resolve_printer_sku() to load printer profiles from printer_profiles.json and populate selected_printer_sku on GlobalState. Finally iterates through all kwargs and sets any additional keys as attributes on GlobalState using setattr(). This method is the main entry point for framework configuration, typically called from the main CLI handler.

Loads locale information from locale_map.json and populates GlobalState with country, region, and language data. Opens Constants.LOCALE_MAP_PATH with UTF-8 encoding and loads the JSON data. Checks if GlobalState.locale exists in the data dictionary - raises ValueError with descriptive message if not found. Retrieves the locale_data dictionary for the specified locale. Sets GlobalState.country to locale_data['country']. Sets GlobalState.country_code to locale_data['code']. Sets GlobalState.region to locale_data['region']. Gets the languages list from locale_data['languages']. Sets GlobalState.language to the provided language or defaults to the first language in the list if not provided. Constructs GlobalState.language_code by concatenating language, hyphen, and country_code (e.g., 'en-US'). This method translates a locale identifier like 'US' into detailed country and language information.

Loads address data from address_data.json and populates GlobalState.address_payload. Opens Constants.ADDRESS_DATA_PATH with UTF-8 encoding and loads the JSON data. Checks if GlobalState.country exists in the data dictionary - raises ValueError with descriptive message if not found. Sets GlobalState.address_payload to the address dictionary for the specified country. This address payload typically contains fields like street, city, state, postal_code, etc., and is used by test flows that need to fill address forms.

Resolves the printer SKU based on printer profile, region, and easy_enroll preference. Returns early if GlobalState.printer_profile is not set. Opens Constants.PRINTER_PROFILES_PATH with UTF-8 encoding and loads the JSON data. Gets the profile value from GlobalState.printer_profile. Checks if the profile exists in the printers dictionary - raises ValueError if not found. Gets the SKUs list for the specified region from printers[profile]['skus'][region] - raises ValueError if no SKUs found for the region. If GlobalState.easy_enroll is not None, converts it to lowercase string and checks if it equals 'yes' to get the desired boolean value. Filters the SKUs list to only include SKUs where the 'easy_enroll' field matches the desired value. Raises ValueError if no SKUs match the easy_enroll preference. Sets GlobalState.selected_printer_sku to the 'sku' field of the first matching SKU. This method selects an appropriate printer SKU based on the test configuration, which is used by test flows that need to select or verify printer models.

Generates and returns the current timestamp as a formatted string in the pattern YYYY_MM_DD_HH_MM_SS. Uses datetime.now() to get the current date and time, then applies strftime formatting with underscores as separators. This timestamp format is commonly used for creating unique filenames, directory names, and run identifiers throughout the framework.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The filename string to sanitize by removing invalid characters

functions.what_it_does: Sanitizes a filename string by replacing all invalid filesystem characters with underscores. Uses a regular expression pattern to match and replace characters that are not allowed in filenames on Windows and other operating systems: backslash, forward slash, asterisk, question mark, colon, double quote, less than, greater than, and pipe. Returns the cleaned filename string safe for use in file operations.

parameters.name: length
parameters.description: The length of the random string to generate. Defaults to 6 characters.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a random alphanumeric string of specified length using lowercase letters and digits. Creates a character pool by combining string.ascii_lowercase (a-z) and string.digits (0-9), then uses random.choices() to randomly select characters from this pool k times where k equals the length parameter. Joins the selected characters into a single string and returns it. Useful for generating unique identifiers, test data, or temporary names.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The text or bytes to hash. Can be a string or bytes object.

functions.what_it_does: Generates a SHA-256 hash of the provided text and returns the first 12 characters of the hexadecimal digest. If the input is already bytes, uses it directly; otherwise converts the text to UTF-8 encoded bytes (treating None as empty string). Computes the SHA-256 hash using hashlib.sha256(), converts to hexadecimal string with hexdigest(), and slices the first 12 characters. This shortened hash is used throughout the framework for creating unique but manageable identifiers for screenshots, files, and cached data.

parameters.name: dirs
parameters.description: Variable number of directory path strings to create

functions.what_it_does: Creates multiple directories if they do not already exist. Accepts a variable number of directory path arguments using *dirs. Iterates through each provided directory path and calls os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True, which creates the directory and any necessary parent directories without raising an error if the directory already exists. This is a convenience function for ensuring output directories exist before writing files.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being accessed

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables attribute-style access to dictionary keys in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is accessed using dot notation (e.g., obj.key_name), this method is called. It attempts to return the value associated with the key from the dictionary using self[name]. If the key does not exist, catches the KeyError and raises an AttributeError with a descriptive message indicating that the attribute does not exist on the ElementsDict object.

parameters.name: self
parameters.description: Instance reference

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: The attribute name being set

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to assign to the attribute

functions.what_it_does: Magic method that enables setting dictionary keys using attribute-style assignment in the ElementsDict class. When an attribute is assigned using dot notation (e.g., obj.key_name = value), this method is called. It stores the value in the dictionary using self[name] = value, effectively treating attribute assignment as dictionary key assignment. This allows the ElementsDict to be used with both dictionary and object syntax interchangeably.

functions.method_signature: def get_or_create_sheet(workbook, sheet_name: str, headers: list = None)

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object to operate on

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to retrieve or create

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: Optional list of header strings to write to the first row. If None, uses Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS.

functions.docstring: Return the sheet by name, or create it (with headers) if missing.
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves an existing Excel worksheet by name from the workbook, or creates a new one if it does not exist. First checks if sheet_name is in workbook.sheetnames. If found, returns the existing sheet. If not found, creates a new sheet using workbook.create_sheet() with the specified title. Then determines the headers to use: either the provided headers parameter or Constants.SCRAPE_HEADERS as default. Iterates through the headers list and writes each header to the first row of the sheet, starting at column 1. Sets horizontal center alignment for each header cell. Returns the sheet object.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet to search

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: The selector value to search for in the first column

functions.docstring: Return True if any value in the first column matches 'selector'.
functions.what_it_does: Searches an Excel worksheet to determine if a specific selector value already exists in the first column. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping the header row) using sheet.iter_rows() with min_row=2, max_col=1, and values_only=True to get only the values from the first column. For each cell value, checks if it equals the provided selector string. Returns True immediately if a match is found. Returns False if no match is found after checking all rows. This is used to prevent duplicate entries when appending scraped data.
functions.usage_example: exists = find_row(sheet, '[data-testid="login-btn"]')  # Returns True or False

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet to append data to

parameters.name: row_data
parameters.description: List of values representing a row to append to the sheet

functions.docstring: Append row_data to the sheet if not already present (by selector).
functions.what_it_does: Appends a row of scraped data to an Excel worksheet only if the selector (first column value) does not already exist in the sheet. Extracts the selector value from row_data[0]. First checks if the selector is empty or if find_row() returns True indicating the selector already exists. If either condition is true, logs a debug message indicating the row is being skipped as duplicate or empty and returns without appending. If the selector is unique and not empty, appends the entire row_data list to the sheet using sheet.append(). This prevents duplicate scraping of the same UI element.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet to adjust column widths for

functions.docstring: Resize columns based on maximum content length for readability.
functions.what_it_does: Automatically adjusts the width of all columns in an Excel worksheet based on the maximum content length in each column for improved readability. Iterates through each column in the sheet using sheet.columns. For each column, gets the column letter using get_column_letter() from the first cell's column index. Iterates through all cells in the column to find the maximum length by converting each cell value to string and measuring its length. Sets the column width to the maximum length plus 2 characters for padding using sheet.column_dimensions[col_letter].width. This ensures all content is visible without truncation.

parameters.name: workbook
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object to search

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: The name of the sheet to retrieve

functions.docstring: Return sheet by name or None (with a warning).
functions.what_it_does: Retrieves an Excel worksheet by name from a workbook, returning None with a warning if not found. First checks if the workbook exists and if sheet_name is in workbook.sheetnames. If both conditions are true, returns the sheet object using workbook[sheet_name]. If the sheet is not found, logs a warning message using framework_logger.warning() indicating the sheet name was not found in the workbook, then returns None. This provides safe sheet access with logging for debugging.
functions.usage_example: sheet = get_sheet_by_name(wb, 'LoginPage')  # Returns sheet or None

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The file path to the Excel workbook

parameters.name: headers
parameters.description: List of header strings (not used in current implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Loads an existing Excel workbook from the specified path or creates a new empty workbook if the file does not exist. First checks if the file exists at the given path using os.path.exists(). If the file exists, loads it using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. If the file does not exist, creates a new Workbook object, removes the default sheet that is automatically created, and sets ws to None. Returns a tuple containing the workbook object and the worksheet object (or None). Note that the headers parameter is accepted but not currently used in the implementation.

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: The openpyxl Workbook object to save

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The file path where the workbook should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Saves an Excel workbook to the specified file path, creating the directory structure if it does not exist. First extracts the directory path from the full file path using os.path.dirname(). If the directory path is not empty, creates all necessary parent directories using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True to avoid errors if directories already exist. Then saves the workbook to the specified path using wb.save(). This ensures the workbook can be saved even if the output directory structure does not yet exist.

parameters.name: excel_path
parameters.description: Path to the Excel file to read contexts from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns a sorted list of unique context names from an Excel file. Loads the workbook from excel_path using openpyxl.load_workbook() and gets the active worksheet. Creates an empty set called contexts to store unique context values. Iterates through all rows starting from row 2 (skipping header) using ws.iter_rows() with min_row=2 and values_only=True. For each row, unpacks the first two values as flow and context. If both flow and context are not empty, adds the context to the set. After processing all rows, converts the set to a sorted list and returns it. This is used to discover all unique test contexts defined in test data files.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to check visibility and enabled state

functions.what_it_does: Checks if a Playwright element is hidden or disabled by evaluating JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to execute JavaScript that checks two conditions: whether el.offsetParent is null (indicating the element is hidden via CSS display:none or visibility:hidden) or whether el.disabled is true (indicating a form element is disabled). Returns True if either condition is met, False otherwise. This is more reliable than simple visibility checks as it detects elements hidden by CSS properties.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to get position information from

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves the position and dimensions of a Playwright element as a dictionary. Calls elem.bounding_box() to get the element's bounding box information, which returns a dictionary with x, y, width, and height keys, or None if the element is not visible. Uses the or operator with an empty dict as fallback if bounding_box returns None. Constructs and returns a new dictionary with integer values for x, y, width, and height coordinates, using box.get() with default value 0 for each property and converting to int. This provides consistent position data even for hidden elements.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract computed styles from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts computed CSS styles from a Playwright element by executing JavaScript in the browser context. Uses elem.evaluate() to run a JavaScript function that gets the computed styles using window.getComputedStyle(el). Constructs and returns a dictionary object containing six style properties: font_family, font_size, font_weight, color, background (backgroundColor), and text_align. Each property uses the logical OR operator to provide an empty string as fallback if the style property is undefined. This is used during scraping to capture visual styling information for validation purposes.

parameters.name: elem
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle to extract text from

functions.what_it_does: Extracts the visible inner text from a Playwright element and returns it as a stripped string. Calls elem.inner_text() to get the text content of the element as it appears to the user (excluding hidden elements and respecting CSS display properties). Then calls strip() on the result to remove leading and trailing whitespace. Returns the cleaned text string. This is the primary method for extracting text content during scraping operations.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object to freeze scrolling on

functions.what_it_does: Disables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to hidden on html and body elements. Uses page.add_style_tag() to inject a style tag with content 'html, body { overflow: hidden !important; }' into the page. The !important flag ensures this style overrides any existing overflow styles. This is useful when capturing screenshots or performing visual comparisons where you want to prevent the page from scrolling during the operation.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object to restore scrolling on

functions.what_it_does: Re-enables scrolling on a web page by injecting CSS that sets overflow to auto on html and body elements. Uses page.add_style_tag() to inject a style tag with content 'html, body { overflow: auto !important; }' into the page. The !important flag ensures this style overrides the previous freeze_scroll style. This restores normal scrolling behavior after it was disabled by freeze_scroll(). Should be called after screenshot or visual comparison operations are complete.

functions.method_signature: def take_element_screenshot(page: Page, selector: str, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object containing the element

parameters.name: selector
parameters.description: CSS selector or other locator string to identify the element

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element on the page identified by a selector string. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. First locates the element using page.locator(selector). Checks if the element count is 0 or if the element is not visible using is_visible(), returning False if either condition is true. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls element.screenshot() with the output path to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True if successful. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the selector and error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False.

functions.method_signature: def take_element_handle_screenshot(handle: ElementHandle, output_path: str) -> bool

parameters.name: handle
parameters.description: Playwright ElementHandle object to capture screenshot of

parameters.name: output_path
parameters.description: File path where the screenshot should be saved

functions.what_it_does: Captures a screenshot of a specific element using an ElementHandle object. Wraps the operation in a try-except block. First checks if the element is visible using handle.is_visible(), returning False if not visible. Extracts the directory path from output_path and creates it if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Calls handle.screenshot() with the output path to capture and save the screenshot. Returns True if successful. If any exception occurs, logs a warning with the error message using framework_logger.warning() and returns False. This is similar to take_element_screenshot but works with ElementHandle objects instead of selector strings.

functions.method_signature: def save_full_page_screenshot(page: Page, flow_name: str, context_name: str, output_dir: str = None) -> str

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page object to capture screenshot of

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow for filename generation

parameters.name: context_name
parameters.description: Name of the context/checkpoint for filename generation

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Optional output directory path. If None, uses Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR.

functions.what_it_does: Captures a full-page screenshot and saves it with a timestamped filename. Determines the output directory using the provided output_dir parameter or Constants.VALIDATE_OUTPUT_DIR as default. Creates the directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs() with exist_ok=True. Generates a filename by combining flow_name, context_name, and current timestamp from get_current_timestamp(), then sanitizes it using sanitize_filename() and appends .png extension. Constructs the full output path by joining the directory and filename. Calls page.screenshot() with the path and full_page=True to capture the entire scrollable page. Returns the filename string (not the full path). This is used during validation to capture baseline and comparison screenshots.

functions.method_signature: def highlight_validation(ws, row_cells, passed: bool = None)

parameters.name: ws
parameters.description: The openpyxl worksheet containing the validation results

parameters.name: row_cells
parameters.description: List of cell objects representing a row to highlight

parameters.name: passed
parameters.description: Optional boolean indicating pass/fail status (not currently used in logic)

functions.what_it_does: Applies color highlighting to validation result rows in Excel based on Text_Result and Screenshot_Result column values. First extracts the header row from the worksheet and creates a dictionary mapping column names to their indices. Defines a helper function norm() that normalizes cell values to uppercase stripped strings. Gets the normalized values of Text_Result and Screenshot_Result columns from the row. If both are empty or N/A, returns without highlighting. Then applies complex conditional logic to determine the appropriate fill color from Constants: PASS (both pass), PASS_TEXT_ONLY (text pass, screenshot N/A), PASS_WITH_EXCLUDES (pass with exclusions), or FAIL (any failure). Finally applies the determined fill color to all cells in the row. This provides visual feedback in validation reports.

functions.method_signature: def generate_report(data: list, output_dir: str, filename: str = "validation_results")

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: List of dictionaries containing validation result data

parameters.name: output_dir
parameters.description: Directory path where report files should be saved

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Base filename for the report files without extension. Defaults to 'validation_results'.

functions.what_it_does: Generates both JSON and HTML validation reports from validation result data. Creates the output directory if it does not exist using os.makedirs(). Constructs file paths for JSON and HTML reports by joining output_dir with filename and appropriate extensions. Writes the data list as formatted JSON to the JSON file using json.dump() with indent=2. Then creates an HTML report by writing HTML structure with a table. If data is not empty, extracts headers from the first dictionary's keys and writes them as table headers. Iterates through each row dictionary and writes table rows with values for each header. Closes the HTML tags. This provides human-readable and machine-parseable validation reports.

functions.method_signature: def write_excel_context(rows, wb, sheet_name, path, header, highlight_func=None)

parameters.name: rows
parameters.description: List of row data to write (can be dicts or lists)

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: Openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: Name of the sheet to write to

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to save the workbook

parameters.name: header
parameters.description: List of header column names

parameters.name: highlight_func
parameters.description: Optional function to call for highlighting each row after writing

functions.what_it_does: Writes validation or scraping data rows to an Excel sheet with header management and optional row highlighting. Gets or creates the sheet by name. If sheet is empty, writes the header row and applies header fill color from Constants.COLOR_HEADER. If sheet has data, validates the first row matches the expected header and replaces it if different. Identifies and deletes empty rows and duplicate header rows. Iterates through the provided rows, skipping empty rows. For each row, if it's a dictionary, extracts values in header order; if it's a list, uses it directly. Appends the row to the sheet. If highlight_func is provided, calls it with the worksheet, the newly added row cells, and None. Finally saves the workbook to the specified path. This is the core function for writing test results to Excel.

parameters.name: results
parameters.description: List of validation result dictionaries

parameters.name: wb
parameters.description: Openpyxl Workbook object

parameters.name: sheet_name
parameters.description: Name of the sheet to write to

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: File path to save the workbook

functions.what_it_does: Convenience wrapper function for writing validation report data to Excel with automatic highlighting. Calls write_excel_context() with the provided results, workbook, sheet_name, and path parameters. Uses Constants.VALIDATION_HEADERS as the header list and highlight_validation as the highlight_func parameter. This ensures validation reports are written with the correct header structure and automatic pass/fail color highlighting based on validation results. Simplifies the common use case of writing validation reports.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: CamelCase string to convert to snake_case

functions.docstring:
Convert CamelCase flow names to snake_case module/function names.
e.g. CreateV3Account -> create_v3_account
functions.what_it_does: Converts CamelCase strings to snake_case format using regular expressions. First applies re.sub() with pattern '(.)([A-Z][a-z]+)' and replacement r'\1_\2' to insert underscores before uppercase letters followed by lowercase letters. Then applies another re.sub() with pattern '([a-z0-9])([A-Z])' and replacement r'\1_\2' to handle transitions from lowercase/digits to uppercase. Finally converts the entire result to lowercase using lower(). This is used to convert flow class names like 'CreateV3Account' to module/function names like 'create_v3_account' for dynamic imports.

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Dictionary of flow arguments containing language/locale information

functions.what_it_does: Extracts and returns the language code from flow arguments dictionary. Uses dict.get() to retrieve the value associated with the key 'tenant_country_language_code' from the flow_args dictionary. Returns the language code string if present, or None if the key does not exist. This is used throughout the framework to determine which language-specific test data and localization to use during test execution.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Target platform identifier (e.g., 'web_chrome', 'GothamDesktop')

parameters.name: simulator_platform
parameters.description: Simulator platform identifier used when target does not start with 'web_'

functions.docstring: Get the target directory name based on target and simulator_platform parameters from flow args.
functions.what_it_does: Determines the appropriate target directory name based on platform identifiers. Defines a comprehensive target_folder_mapping dictionary that maps various platform identifiers to standardized folder names (e.g., 'web_chrome' to 'chrome', 'GothamDesktop' to 'wingotham', 'Android' to 'android', etc.). Checks if the target parameter starts with 'web_'. If yes, looks up the target in the mapping dictionary and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. If target does not start with 'web_', looks up simulator_platform in the mapping and returns the mapped value or the original target if not found. This standardizes directory naming across different execution platforms.

functions.method_signature: def run_flow(mode: str, flow_name: str, flow_args: Optional[Dict] = None, context_override: Optional[str] = None) -> None

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Execution mode: 'scrape', 'validate', or 'noop'

parameters.name: flow_name
parameters.description: Name of the test flow to execute

parameters.name: flow_args
parameters.description: Optional dictionary of flow arguments including locale, target, etc.

parameters.name: context_override
parameters.description: Optional context name override (not currently used in implementation)

functions.what_it_does: Core flow execution orchestrator that dynamically loads and runs test flows with scrape, validate, or noop modes. Validates flow_name is provided. Initializes flow_args to empty dict if None. Converts flow_name to snake_case module name using camel_to_snake(). Constructs module path as 'test_flows.{flow_name}.{module_basename}'. Dynamically imports the flow module using importlib.import_module(), catching ModuleNotFoundError and logging errors. Validates the module has a function matching module_basename. Extracts configuration from flow_args including run_id timestamp, lang_code, target platform, and log collection settings. Based on mode, sets up appropriate output directories and defines a stage_callback function that calls either scrape_context or validate_context. Initializes Config with all flow arguments. Optionally starts HTML report generation. Executes the flow function with stage_callback, catching exceptions and marking flow as failed. Finalizes the report. Checks for step failures or interruptions and raises SystemExit(1) if validation failed. Logs completion message with output directory. This is the central flow execution engine.
